title: Sweat Stains on Wool
stainChemistry: Sweat on wool is complex because wool itself is protein fiber (keratin), and sweat contains proteins (urea, salts, shed skin cells). Additionally, wool has natural affinity for both water (from urea and salts) and oils (body oils), leading to oxidative yellowing over time. Yellowing on wool occurs as body oils oxidize (Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ in iron-containing compounds from blood/rust). Wool's protein structure is delicate—protease enzymes digest wool's keratin along with sweat proteins, destroying fiber strength permanently. Temperature is critical: heat felts wool irreversibly. Dyes in colored wool are heat-sensitive. The approach must be extremely conservative.
whyThisWorks: For wool, Protein must be avoided entirely—no Protein, ever. Instead, mild NSD surfactant + cool water + minimal mechanical action removes fresh sweat. For yellowing (oxidized oils), very dilute H₂O₂ on white wool only can help reverse oxidation. For colored wool, accept that yellow/brown discoloration may be permanent without professional solvent treatment. Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) is an option for white wool but requires professional expertise.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle or running cool water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse gently under cool running water. This removes salt, urea, and some loose sweat residue from wool's surface. Do NOT rub or agitate—wool fibers snag and pill immediately under mechanical stress. Blot only, pressing gently with cloth. For fresh sweat stains, this rinse alone may be sufficient to remove the stain completely.

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD solution (wool-specific detergent, pH-neutral, NO enzymes)
spottingProtocol.equipment: Basin with cool water and wool detergent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix pH-neutral wool detergent (NSD, Dreft) at . Immerse stained area gently. Wool tolerates mild surfactant action better than enzymes. Detergent molecules encapsulate sweat protein residue and suspend it. Keep temperature below 30°C (cold, never tepid). Keep hands still—no mechanical agitation. After 5-10 minutes, rinse immediately.

spottingProtocol.agent: Soft cloth with gentle pressure only
spottingProtocol.technique: Blot ONLY, no rubbing or scrubbing
spottingProtocol.instruction: With very soft cloth, dab stain GENTLY. DO NOT SCRUB OR RUB—wool fibers snag and pill immediately with friction. Press gently—the surfactant is doing the work, you're just blotting away loosened sweat protein. Light pressure only.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle or running cool water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse gently under cool running water until no soap remains. Blot gently with cloth to absorb excess water. Thoroughness prevents soap residue that attracts dirt later.

spottingProtocol.agent: Optional: Weak vinegar rinse (1 tbsp per liter cool water) for cuticle smoothing
spottingProtocol.equipment: Basin with weakly acidified cool water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Optional professional technique: final weak vinegar rinse helps smooth wool's cuticles and prevents felting. Gently squeeze (never wring) and proceed to drying. This step improves final appearance.

spottingProtocol.agent: Optional: Dilute H₂O₂ for yellowing on WHITE WOOL ONLY
spottingProtocol.equipment: Hidden test area, 3% H₂O₂ from drugstore
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: TEST 1-2 minutes on hidden seam first. If safe: 3-5 minutes on stain maximum
spottingProtocol.instruction: For white wool only—if yellowing remains after detergent rinse, you can try very dilute H₂O₂ . ALWAYS test on hidden seam first for 1-2 minutes. Check for yellowing or weakening. If safe, apply to stain for maximum 5 minutes (never longer—oxidative action risks yellowing itself). DO NOT use on colored wool—bleaching risk. Blot immediately, rinse thoroughly with cool water.

homeSolutions.agent: Wool + sweat: fresh resolves gracefully, yellow buildup is pro territory
homeSolutions.instruction: Sweat on wool has the same four parts as sweat on any other fabric: protein, oil, salt, and (if antiperspirant) aluminum. Fresh sweat on wool often rinses out with cool water alone. Yellow buildup on wool (weeks of antiperspirant + sweat) is almost always pro territory because home peroxide is limited to a strict 60-second ceiling on wool and that's rarely enough to finish old aluminum-protein complexes. Know which camp you're in before you start — it saves 20 minutes of hopeful scrubbing.

Wool bans: no enzymes (digest wool keratin — and most retail enzyme cleaners are blends you can't verify), no hot water, no chlorine bleach, no alkaline (pH > 7 opens scales and triggers felting), no scrubbing or wringing. Sweat-specific: vinegar is the home tool for aluminum-protein pit staining. It's wool-safe within the 60-second ceiling and does real work on the exact chemistry behind yellow marks.

Dab cool water on an inside seam, wait 60 seconds, blot. If clean, proceed. Press a white cloth straight down on the stain. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in. Keep going until the cloth stops picking up yellow. Never rub — wool felts fast under wet friction.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool water rinse for fresh sweat
homeSolutions.instruction: For fresh sweat only (same day, no visible yellow): turn the garment inside out and run cool water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Press between dry towels. This alone often removes most of a fresh pit stain on wool. If you're seeing yellow, skip ahead to step 5.

Mix one teaspoon of Eucalan, Woolite, or Soak (NEVER OxiClean or "bio" detergents) in two cups of cool water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, press on the stain. 3–5 minutes dwell. Blot. Follow with 1 part white vinegar + 1 part cool water dabbed on the spot for 60 SECONDS MAX — set a timer. Rinse with cool water. Vinegar breaks aluminum-protein and restores wool's pH at the same time.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never combine vinegar with bleach or peroxide on the same dwell.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% peroxide dab — STRICT 60-sec ceiling, white wool only
homeSolutions.instruction: If yellow remains AND the wool is white or cream AND your patch test came back clean: mix 1 part 3% peroxide with 1 part cool water. Dab with a cotton swab for 60 SECONDS MAX, flush with cool water immediately. Peroxide yellows wool at longer dwells. Skip entirely on colored wool — accept that yellowing may be permanent without professional help.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never exceed 60 seconds peroxide on wool. Never combine with ammonia or bleach.

homeSolutions.agent: Press dry + lay flat + don't double-dose
homeSolutions.instruction: Press between dry white towels to lift moisture. Lay flat, reshape while damp. Air-dry at room temperature, out of direct sun. Never hang wet wool. Never apply heat. If yellow persists after one round, DO NOT repeat the full protocol — repeat chemistry is the #1 cause of felting damage on wool. Escalate instead.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a delicates specialist for set yellow
homeSolutions.instruction: Yellow pit-stain buildup on wool that survives one round is professional territory. Tell them: "Sweat + antiperspirant yellowing on wool, age [x]. Treated at home with Eucalan + 1:1 vinegar + [60-sec peroxide on white] — one round, strict dwells. No enzymes, no bleach, no alkaline, no heat." Pros use reducing bleach with wool-safe technique that outperforms any home dwell window.

materialWarnings: NEVER Protein enzymes or household enzyme pre-treaters—they digest wool's keratin fiber. Forbidden entirely.
NEVER hot water above 30°C—wool felts permanently. Irreversible damage.
NEVER rub, scrub, or agitate—wool fibers snag and pill immediately.
NEVER use heat to dry (dryer, iron)—permanent felting.
NEVER use bleach on colored wool—dye damage. Oxygen bleach also damages wool protein.
NEVER hang to dry—gravity stretches wet wool.
escalation: Sweat stains on wool rarely require professional help if treated when fresh. However, if stain has oxidized to yellow/brown (months old), professional dry cleaning is recommended. Professionals can assess whether discoloration is protein-based (removable with solvent) or true oxidized iron (may be permanent). They have solvent-based spotting equipment to safely treat without felting risk. For valuable wool or cashmere, professional assessment is safest.

household.name: Wool-Safe Detergent (pH-neutral, enzyme-free)
household.note: Enzyme-free, pH-neutral wool wash. Household substitute for professional NSD. pH-neutral, no enzymes. Designed to protect wool fiber. Use minimally and at cool temperature.

household.name: Dreft
household.note: Gentle, pH-neutral. No enzymes. Safe for wool protein fiber.

household.name: White Vinegar (optional)
household.note: Optional final rinse aid for cuticle smoothing. Prevents felting. 1 tbsp per liter. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: H₂O₂ 3% (optional, white wool only)
household.note: For yellowing on white wool only. Test first on hidden area. Never use on colored wool.

professional.name: Professional Dry Cleaning (solvent-based)
professional.note: For aged or oxidized sweat stains on wool. Professionals assess stain type and use solvent-based spotting to avoid felting.

customerExplanation: Sweat stains on wool require extreme caution because wool itself is protein fiber (keratin). We avoided enzymes entirely—protease would damage wool's structure. We used very mild, neutral-pH detergent and kept everything cold to prevent felting. For fresh sweat, cool water rinse alone works. For older sweat, we added gentle detergent wash. For yellowing, we offered very dilute H₂O₂ on white wool only. Air dry flat—never heat, which would felt the wool permanently.
commonMistakes: Using Protein pre-treaters or enzyme products—they digest wool's keratin protein. Absolutely forbidden.
Using hot water—even 35°C triggers felting. Permanent damage.
Rubbing or scrubbing—wool fibers lock together immediately under friction.
Using heat to dry—wool felts permanently above 30°C.
Using bleach on colored wool—dye damage and fiber weakening.
Hanging wet wool to dry—gravity stretches the garment.
Repeated treatment for persistent yellowing—cumulative damage risk.
scienceNote: Wool's keratin has a triple-helix structure held together by hydrogen bonds and disulfide bonds. Cuticle scales overlap. Under neutral pH and cool water, scales lie flat. When pH rises above 7 (alkalinity) or temperature exceeds 30°C, scales swell and interlock (felting)—irreversible structural change. Protein enzymes are absolutely forbidden because they cleave peptide bonds in the triple helix, destroying fiber strength permanently. For yellowing: body oils oxidize over months (Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ in traces of iron), forming brown/yellow oxidized compounds. Very dilute H₂O₂ can reduce some oxidized iron, but extended oxidation may be permanent.
callPro: For aged, oxidized sweat stains on wool, professional dry cleaning is recommended. Professionals assess whether discoloration is removable (protein-based residue vs. true oxidation) and have solvent-based equipment to safely treat without felting risk. For fine merino wool, cashmere, or valuable garments, professional assessment prevents accidental damage from home chemistry.
solventNote: Wool is more solvent-stable than water-based treatment because organic solvents bypass the pH and temperature sensitivities that trigger felting. Professionals rely on solvent-based spotting for wool sweat stains—zero felting risk compared to water chemistry.
aftercare: Lay wool garment flat on clean towel. Do NOT hang (gravity stretches wet wool and may distort shape permanently). Do NOT use heat (dryer, iron)—wool felts at temperature above 30°C. Open air circulation is ideal. Once completely dry, inspect. If yellowing persists, accept it—repeated treatment risks cumulative damage. For persistent yellowing, professional dry cleaning with optimized solvent-based treatment is the next step.
